questions.stem: Why does gas exchange become more challenging as an organism gets larger?
questions.explanation: As size increases, the surface area-to-volume ratio falls and diffusion distances increase, making exchange harder.

questions.stem: Which is a property of an effective gas-exchange surface?

options.text: A large, thin, moist, permeable surface

questions.explanation: Gas-exchange surfaces are permeable, thin, moist and have a large surface area.

questions.stem: How do animals maintain concentration gradients at gas-exchange surfaces?

options.text: By dense blood vessel networks, blood flow and ventilation

questions.explanation: Dense capillary networks, continuous blood flow and ventilation maintain steep concentration gradients.

questions.stem: Which is an adaptation of mammalian alveolar lungs for gas exchange?

options.text: A high surface area with extensive capillary beds
A single large air sac with no capillaries

questions.explanation: Alveolar lungs have surfactant, branched bronchioles, extensive capillary beds and a high surface area.

questions.stem: Surfactant in the alveoli functions to:

options.text: Reduce surface tension so alveoli do not collapse

questions.explanation: Surfactant lowers surface tension, helping keep alveoli open for efficient gas exchange.

questions.stem: What happens to the diaphragm's shape?
questions.explanation: The diaphragm contracts and flattens during inhalation, increasing the volume of the thorax.

questions.stem: Which muscles, together with the diaphragm, are involved in ventilating the lungs?
questions.explanation: The diaphragm, intercostal muscles, abdominal muscles and ribs all take part in ventilation.

questions.scenario: A student measures the maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after the deepest possible inhalation.
questions.stem: Which lung volume is this?
questions.explanation: Vital capacity is the maximum volume that can be exhaled after the deepest inhalation.

questions.stem: Tidal volume is the volume of air:

options.text: Breathed in or out during normal quiet breathing

questions.explanation: Tidal volume is the amount of air moved in or out with each normal breath.

questions.stem: Which leaf feature is an adaptation for gas exchange?

options.text: Air spaces in the spongy mesophyll
A thick waxy layer blocking all gases

questions.explanation: Leaf adaptations for gas exchange include air spaces, spongy mesophyll and stomatal guard cells.

questions.stem: Gases enter and leave a leaf mainly through pores controlled by:
questions.explanation: Stomata, controlled by guard cells, are the main route for gas exchange in leaves.

questions.scenario: A student draws a plan diagram of a transverse section of a leaf.
questions.stem: A plan diagram should show:

options.text: The distribution of tissues, without individual cells
Only the colour of the leaf

questions.explanation: A plan diagram shows the distribution of tissues in the section rather than individual cells.

questions.scenario: Open stomata allow gas exchange but also let water escape.
questions.stem: This loss of water vapour from a leaf is called:
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the loss of water vapour that occurs as a consequence of gas exchange through stomata.

questions.stem: Which factor is likely to increase the rate of transpiration?
questions.explanation: Higher temperature increases transpiration, while high humidity and darkness reduce it.

questions.scenario: A student counts stomata in several fields of view under the microscope.
questions.stem: Why should the count be repeated in different fields of view?

options.text: One count is always perfectly accurate
Repeating wastes time with no benefit

questions.explanation: Repeating measurements increases the reliability of quantitative data given natural variability in biological material.

questions.stem: Stomatal density is a measure of the:

options.text: Number of stomata per unit area of leaf

questions.explanation: Stomatal density is the number of stomata per unit area, determined from micrographs or leaf casts.

questions.stem: Why must gas-exchange surfaces be moist?

options.text: Gases dissolve in the moisture before diffusing across

questions.explanation: A moist surface allows gases to dissolve so they can diffuse across the exchange surface.

questions.scenario: Fish ventilate their gills with water.
questions.stem: Ventilation helps gas exchange by:

options.text: Maintaining a steep concentration gradient at the surface

questions.explanation: Ventilation (with water for gills, air for lungs) refreshes the surface, maintaining concentration gradients.

questions.stem: The branched network of bronchioles in the lungs helps gas exchange by:

options.text: Delivering air to a very large alveolar surface area

questions.explanation: Branched bronchioles distribute air across the many alveoli, giving a high surface area for exchange.

questions.stem: The waxy cuticle of a leaf functions mainly to:

options.text: Reduce water loss from the leaf surface

questions.explanation: The waxy cuticle reduces water loss; gas exchange occurs mainly through the stomata.

questions.scenario: A very small single-celled organism exchanges gases directly with its surroundings.
questions.stem: Why can it do this without special exchange organs?

options.text: It has a large surface area-to-volume ratio and short diffusion distances
It has a very small surface area

questions.explanation: Small organisms have a high surface area-to-volume ratio and short diffusion distances, so simple diffusion suffices.

questions.scenario: During exhalation at rest, the diaphragm and external intercostals relax.
questions.stem: What happens to the volume of the thorax?
questions.explanation: Relaxation reduces thoracic volume, raising pressure so air is exhaled.

questions.stem: The inspiratory reserve volume is the extra air that can be:

options.text: Left in the lungs after full exhalation

questions.explanation: The inspiratory reserve is the additional air that can be inhaled beyond a normal tidal breath.

questions.scenario: A transverse section of a dicotyledonous leaf is examined.
questions.stem: Which tissues would be labelled on the plan diagram?
questions.explanation: A leaf plan diagram shows tissues such as the epidermis, mesophyll layers and veins.

questions.stem: Which factor would decrease the rate of transpiration?
questions.explanation: High humidity reduces the water vapour gradient, decreasing transpiration; heat, wind and light tend to increase it.

questions.stem: A thin tissue layer at a gas-exchange surface is important because it:

options.text: Shortens the diffusion distance for gases

questions.explanation: A thin surface reduces the distance gases must diffuse, speeding exchange.

questions.stem: Extensive capillary beds around the alveoli help gas exchange by:

options.text: Carrying blood close to the exchange surface to maintain gradients

questions.explanation: Dense capillaries bring blood close to the alveoli and, with continuous flow, maintain steep gas gradients.

questions.stem: The spongy mesophyll of a leaf is adapted for gas exchange because it:

options.text: Contains many air spaces increasing internal surface area

questions.explanation: Air spaces in the spongy mesophyll increase internal surface area and allow gases to diffuse to and from cells.

questions.stem: Gas exchange is described as a vital function because:

options.text: All organisms need to exchange gases for metabolism

questions.explanation: Gas exchange is vital in all organisms because cells require gases such as oxygen and produce carbon dioxide.

questions.stem: The expiratory reserve volume is the extra air that can be:

options.text: Exhaled after a normal breath out
Inhaled after a normal breath in

questions.explanation: The expiratory reserve is the additional air that can be exhaled beyond a normal tidal breath.

questions.stem: Continuous blood flow at an exchange surface helps by:

options.text: Carrying away absorbed gases to maintain gradients
Allowing gases to build up and stop diffusion

questions.explanation: Continuous blood flow removes absorbed gases and brings fresh blood, keeping concentration gradients steep.

questions.scenario: A student makes a leaf cast to study stomata.
questions.stem: A leaf cast is used to:

options.text: Produce an impression of the leaf surface for counting stomata

questions.explanation: A leaf cast makes an impression of the surface so stomata can be counted to find stomatal density.

questions.stem: The ribs contribute to ventilation by:

options.text: Moving to change the volume of the thoracic cavity

questions.explanation: Movement of the ribs (with the intercostal muscles) changes thoracic volume during ventilation.

questions.stem: A large surface area at a gas-exchange surface allows:

options.text: More gas to diffuse in a given time

questions.explanation: A large surface area increases the amount of gas that can diffuse across in a given time.

questions.stem: Transpiration is best described as a consequence of:

options.text: Active pumping of water out of leaves

questions.explanation: Because stomata must open for gas exchange, water vapour is inevitably lost by transpiration.

questions.stem: Which combination lists adaptations of mammalian alveolar lungs?

options.text: Surfactant, branched bronchioles, extensive capillaries, high surface area
Thick walls, no capillaries, small surface area
Waxy cuticle, guard cells, spongy mesophyll

questions.explanation: Alveolar lung adaptations include surfactant, branched bronchioles, extensive capillary beds and a high surface area.

questions.stem: As an organism increases in size, the distance from its centre to its surface:
questions.explanation: Greater size increases internal distances, so larger organisms need specialized gas-exchange systems.

questions.stem: Which leaf tissue transports water and dissolved substances?
questions.explanation: Veins contain vascular tissue (xylem and phloem) that transport water and dissolved substances in the leaf.

questions.stem: Which apparatus is typically used to measure lung volumes such as tidal volume?
questions.explanation: Lung volumes like tidal volume and vital capacity are commonly measured with a spirometer.

questions.stem: Which structures maintain concentration gradients specifically in the lungs?

options.text: Ventilation with air and dense blood vessels

questions.explanation: In lungs, ventilation with air plus dense blood vessels and continuous flow maintain gradients (gills use water).

questions.stem: Cooperative binding of oxygen to haemoglobin means that:

options.text: Binding of one oxygen makes further binding easier
Each haem group works independently with no effect on others

questions.explanation: Cooperative binding means that as oxygen binds to one haem group, the affinity of the others increases.

questions.stem: Foetal haemoglobin differs from adult haemoglobin in that it has a:
questions.explanation: Foetal haemoglobin has a higher affinity for oxygen, allowing it to take up oxygen from the mother's blood.

questions.scenario: Actively respiring tissue produces a lot of carbon dioxide.
questions.stem: According to the Bohr shift, this causes haemoglobin to:
questions.explanation: The Bohr shift: increased carbon dioxide causes increased dissociation of oxygen, delivering more to active tissues.

questions.stem: Why is the Bohr shift beneficial?

options.text: It delivers more oxygen where respiration is fastest
It reduces oxygen supply to active tissues

questions.explanation: By releasing oxygen where carbon dioxide is high, the Bohr shift supplies more oxygen to actively respiring tissues.

questions.scenario: An oxygen dissociation curve for haemoglobin is S-shaped (sigmoidal).
questions.stem: The S-shape is explained by:
questions.explanation: The sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve results from cooperative binding of oxygen to the haem groups.

questions.scenario: An oxygen dissociation curve plots percentage saturation against oxygen concentration.
questions.stem: At high oxygen concentrations, haemoglobin is:
questions.explanation: The dissociation curve shows haemoglobin becoming highly saturated at high oxygen concentrations, as in the lungs.

questions.stem: Carbon dioxide binding to haemoglobin is described as allosteric because it:

options.text: Binds at a site other than the oxygen-binding site and affects oxygen affinity
Binds at the same site as oxygen
Has no effect on the protein

questions.explanation: Allosteric binding of carbon dioxide occurs at a different site and alters haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen.

questions.scenario: On a graph, increased carbon dioxide shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to the right.
questions.stem: A rightward shift indicates that haemoglobin:
questions.explanation: A rightward (Bohr) shift means oxygen is released more readily, benefiting actively respiring tissues.

questions.stem: The higher oxygen affinity of foetal haemoglobin allows the foetus to:

options.text: Take up oxygen from the mother's blood across the placenta

questions.explanation: With higher affinity, foetal haemoglobin can load oxygen from maternal haemoglobin at the placenta.

questions.stem: The oxygen dissociation curve is a way of representing haemoglobin's:

options.text: Affinity for oxygen at different oxygen concentrations

questions.explanation: The oxygen dissociation curve shows how haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen varies with oxygen concentration.
